 Plan: Session 63 knock-out — scanner hardening, verify fix, CSTA hand-curation, wrap

 Context

 End of Session 62 shipped the rule scanner (R19 + R20) as a hook pipeline, but Commit 0 was deferred to Session 63. Session 63 so far has:

 1. Completed Commit 0 empirical verification — confirmed Stop-block forces in-turn continuation via synthetic user message, User Prompt Submit additional Context reaches Claude's next turn, and the Stop stdin

 field is last_assistant_message (not last_message ).

 2. Fixed a production bug in ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh — it was reading .last_message and silently fail-opening since install. Patched to .last_assistant_message .

 3. Loaded the Wednesday diocesan drip batch one day early on Tue 2026-04-14 per Steven's approval. 15 of 15 processed (measured from the execute summary): 14 created, 1 existing reused, 0 failed, 0 skipped.

 4. Discovered a residual verify mismatch on one diocesan sequence (Philadelphia) — get_sequence_states threw an exception, caller stamped -1 as sentinel. Writes were unaffected; this is a read-path issue only.

 5. Discovered a false-positive in the rule scanner pipeline — the last correction directive flagged three percent tokens ( 17%,40% 20% ) that I cannot locate in my actual text. Root cause is most likely stale

 violation-log entries from either an earlier smoke test this session or leftover state from Session 62. Hooks are currently disabled via kill switch ( ~/.claude/state/scout-hooks-disabled ) while this plan runs

 to prevent further false correction fires during plan-mode work that will contain many legitimate numerical claims.

 Steven's instruction for the rest of Session 63: knock out as much as possible in the order I recommend, EXCLUDING (a) Research Engine Round 1.1 planning, (b) BUG 5 code fix in research_engine.py , and (c)

 Thursday diocesan drip (runs on actual Thu). Everything else that can ship without touching the research engine should be done this session.

 Scope — four tasks, in recommended order

 Task 1 — Scanner infrastructure hardening and re-enable

 Problem. The current wrapper hardcodes LOG=~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log . Smoke-testing the wrapper with a bare-number input writes real entries to the real log, which then cause apparent false

 positives on the next user turn (the S63 "ghost 17%" incident and likely the root cause of the 40% / 20% ghosts as well). The memory file feedback_rule_scanner_hook_installed.md already documents the pattern;

 now the wrapper itself needs to support a safe test mode.

 Changes to ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh (machine-local, not in repo):

 - Replace the hardcoded assignment

 LOG=~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log

 - with an env-var override

 LOG= "${SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG:-$HOME/.claude/state/scout-violations.log}"

 - Same pattern for the kill-switch path would be nice-to-have but is NOT being changed in this session (it's not the root of the smoke-test trap).

 Changes to ~/.claude/hooks/scout-violation-inject.sh (machine-local):

 - Same env-var override for LOG and PROC so a test run with SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG=/tmp/foo.log exercises the full write→inject pipeline without touching production state. The file should fall back to the same

 default when the env var is unset.

 Clear the real violation log state.

 - rm -f ~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log ~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log.processing

 - This guarantees no stale S62 entries poison the first live run after re-enable.

 Smoke-test the hardened wrappers with SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG=/tmp/test-vlog.log :

 1. SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG=/tmp/test-vlog.log bash ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh <<< '{"last_assistant_message":"17%","stop_hook_active":false}' — expect block JSON on stdout and a new line in

 /tmp/test-vlog.log .

 2. SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG=/tmp/test-vlog.log bash ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh <<< '{"last_assistant_message":"17% (measured)","stop_hook_active":false}' — expect empty stdout and no new line.

 3. SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG=/tmp/test-vlog.log bash ~/.claude/hooks/scout-violation-inject.sh <<< '{}' — expect additionalContext JSON mentioning "17%".

 4. Confirm ~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log is absent throughout (writes went to /tmp/test-vlog.log ).

 5. rm -f /tmp/test-vlog.log /tmp/test-vlog.log.processing to clean up.

 Re-enable hooks.

 - rm ~/.claude/state/scout-hooks-disabled .

 - From this point onward every turn in the session is subject to R19 and R20 enforcement with a clean log.

 - /Users/stevenadkins/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh

 - /Users/stevenadkins/.claude/hooks/scout-violation-inject.sh

 - /Users/stevenadkins/.claude/projects/-Users-stevenadkins-Code-Scout/memory/feedback_rule_scanner_hook_installed.md (append smoke-test env-var instructions to the "Smoke-test log-poisoning trap" section

 already in the file)

 - scripts/rule_scanner.py — the scanner itself is correct; all false positives trace to wrapper-level log-hygiene, not scan logic.

 - The label_roots , number_patterns , or label_window_chars in any rule. No rule-set changes.

 Task 2 — Philadelphia -1 verify investigation

 Problem. scripts/diocesan_drip.py --verify --force-day 2026-04-15 returned -1 for the Philadelphia sequence, which is the sentinel for " get_sequence_states threw an exception." All five other sequences matched

 expected live counts. Writes were unaffected (execute summary confirms 15 of 15 POSTed), so this is a read-path issue only, but it does need root-cause before the final verify pass at end-of-session is

 meaningful.

 Diagnostic steps, in order:

 1. Re-run verify with visible warnings. The diocesan_drip cmd_verify already logs the exception via logger.warning(f"seq {sid} live count error: {e}") at scripts/diocesan_drip.py:401 . Re-run --verify

 --force-day 2026-04-15 and capture both stdout and stderr to see the full exception message.

 2. Trace into tools/outreach_client.get_sequence_states (line 459–530-ish). The function does manual pagination with max_pages=100 and page[size]=50 , so 5,000 states is the hard ceiling. Philadelphia's

 sequence is unlikely to have that many, but the exception could come from:

 - Rate limit (HTTP 429 raised by _api_get ) — transient, would resolve on retry.

 - A malformed "next" link in one page's response — surfaces as a urllib.parse error.

 - A JSON decode failure on a truncated response — surfaces as a json.JSONDecodeError.

 - Server-side 500 on a specific page — transient.

 3. Decision tree based on what the warning shows:

 - Transient (429 / 500): add retry-on-429/500 with backoff inside get_sequence_states , then re-run verify to confirm. Small, surgical change.

 - Pagination parse bug: fix the specific parse path; add a regression test.

 - >100-page ceiling hit: raise max_pages to e.g. 500 with a warning log at the cap, and note it in the memory file.

 - Non-reproducible: leave it as a one-off hiccup, document in CURRENT STATE, move on. All writes verified by the execute summary anyway.

 - /Users/stevenadkins/Code/Scout/tools/outreach_client.py — narrowly, only if the diagnosis points to a reproducible bug.

 - /Users/stevenadkins/Code/Scout/scripts/diocesan_drip.py — probably NOT touched; cmd_verify already catches and logs correctly.

 Explicit non-goal. No rewrite of get_sequence_states . Surgical fix only.

 Task 3 — IN / OK / TN CSTA hand-curation

 Problem. Per memory/project_csta_roster_hand_curation_gaps.md : the IN / OK / TN CSTA chapters have zero roster matches against Scout's territory districts because those chapter subdomains exist but don't

 publish their board rosters in a scrapeable format. The S57 audit flagged this as a known gap. Hand-curation can add ~15 matchable prospects (extrapolation based on the S57 audit pattern; actual yield is

 unknown until the boards are read).

 Approach — lightweight, read-only web reads with hand-entered JSON:

 1. Read the memory file project_csta_roster_hand_curation_gaps.md for the exact chapter URLs and any field contract the existing CSTA roster loader expects.

 2. Locate the existing CSTA roster data file (likely memory/csta_rosters.json or similar — to be identified during Task 1's prep reading).

 3. For each of IN / OK / TN:

 - Visit the chapter's official website (URLs stored in the memory file).

 - Read the "Board" / "Leadership" / "About Us" page.

 - Extract each board member's name, school affiliation, role, and email if publicly listed.

 - Skip entries that don't have a school affiliation or are duplicates.

 4. Write the new rosters to the existing seed file using the same schema as the other state chapters.

 5. Run the CSTA queue function ( signal_processor.scan_csta_chapters per tools/CLAUDE.md — sync, ~$1.20/scan) limited to IN/OK/TN to exercise the new rows and queue any newly-matchable prospects as signals.

 6. Report the yield: how many rows were added and how many new prospects were queued.

 - memory/csta_rosters.json (or whichever file is the seed) — additive rows only for IN / OK / TN.

 - None of the scanner code itself — the whole point is that the scanner already works, it just has missing input data.

 Time estimate. 30–60 minutes (estimate — real wall clock depends on how cleanly each chapter website lists its board). If TN alone takes 40 minutes because the board page is a PDF or images, pause and reassess

 rather than sinking the whole session into one state.

 Cost. Web reads are free (WebFetch). The CSTA scan at end is ~$1.20 (measurement from tools/CLAUDE.md docstring).

 Explicit non-goal. No code changes to the CSTA scanner or the territory matching logic. Data-entry-only task.

 Problem. CLAUDE.md CURRENT STATE needs to be the source of truth for "where Session 64 picks up." Session 63 has been heavy — Commit 0, the wrapper bug, the early Wed drip, scanner hardening, a verify

 investigation, and (potentially) CSTA hand-curation. Without a proper wrap, Session 64 starts blind.

 labels (measured / sample / etc.) since the hooks will be live during this write.

 3. Update the header line of CLAUDE.md to reflect Session 63 progress.

 5. Re-verify hooks are live with a final synthetic test: echo '{"last_assistant_message":"status: 99% (measured)","stop_hook_active":false}' | bash ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh — expect empty stdout

 (clean).

 6. Task list cleanup. Mark #1 (Thu drip) as still pending (deferred to actual Thu), #2 (Philadelphia verify) completed or documented as one-off, #3 (EOS wrap) as the task in progress during this step.

 Verification plan

 End-to-end, after all four tasks complete:

 1. Hooks live with clean log — a response containing 99% (measured) passes; a response containing bare 99% triggers a correction. Both verified with the synthetic-stdin smoke test at the end of Task 4.

 Rollback

 Time estimate (honest, not wishful)

 - Task 1 (scanner hardening + re-enable): 15 min estimate

 - Task 2 (verify investigation + optional fix): 20 min estimate (most of the time is in reading the exception and deciding; the code fix is tiny if needed)

 Total: 85–115 min estimate. Current session ctx headroom is 32% to EOS as of the last marker (measured). Task 3 can be truncated (e.g. only IN this session, OK/TN deferred) if time runs short.

 [<u[>1u[>4;2m

 Refine this plan. Your job is to pressure-test it honestly — keep what's
 genuinely strong, rebuild what isn't, and hand back something I can execute
 with full confidence.
 Act as a ruthless, senior-level technical reviewer and architect. Be direct,

 specific, and opinionated. No hedging, no diplomacy, no soft language around

 real problems. If something is wrong, say it's wrong and say why. But the

 inverse also holds: if something is right, leave it alone. Do not rewrite solid

 work just to look thorough — churn for its own sake is its own kind of

 failure. The bar for changing something is that the new version is meaningfully

 better, not merely different.

 happen because the reviewer started reacting before they finished

 understanding.

 1. Challenge the foundation. Is the overall approach actually right, or is it

 just the first thing that came to mind? Interrogate the architecture, the

 framing, and the core assumptions before touching details. If there's a

 fundamentally better angle — different architecture, different sequencing,

 different tradeoffs, a smaller problem hiding inside the big one — say so and

 restructure around it. Do not anchor to your first answer because you already

 committed to it on the page.

 2. Stress-test every layer as if you're executing it tomorrow. Walk the plan

 end to end and ask: could I actually sit down and build this without hitting a

 wall? Find the hidden assumptions, the vague steps, the dependency tangles, the

 places where things will quietly break at the edges, the spots where "it'll

 just work" is doing real load-bearing work. For every problem: name it, explain

 why it happens, explain what it costs, and give the fix. Diagnosis without

 prescription is useless.

 3. Pressure-test every technical decision against the best available knowledge.

 Architecture, tooling, patterns, data modeling, sequencing — for each one, ask

 whether it's genuinely the strongest option or just the most familiar. Weigh

 it against what actually holds up in production, not what's popular in

 tutorials or trending this quarter. Where real tradeoffs exist between

 competing approaches, surface them honestly and take a clear position on which

 one I should pick and why. No "it depends" cop-outs.

 4. Optimize for the highest-quality result, reached as fast and efficiently as

 possible. Every step should be pulling its weight toward a correct, durable,

 high-quality outcome — not just toward motion. Ruthlessly cut anything that

 adds time without adding quality, and ruthlessly add anything that protects

 quality even if it costs a little time upfront. Sequence the work so that the

 decisions and pieces with the highest leverage on the final result come first,

 so that mistakes get caught as early and cheaply as possible, and so that

 nothing downstream has to be redone because something upstream was rushed.

 Where two paths exist, pick the one that gets to the correct answer with the

 least wasted motion — and say why. Speed comes from not doing the wrong work,

 not from skipping the right work.

 5. Cut the fat, keep the muscle. Depth is welcome. Redundancy, vagueness, and

 ceremony are not. Every element earns its place or it goes. If a step would

 make me stop and ask "what does this actually mean in practice," either make it

 concrete or delete it. If two steps do the same work, collapse them. If a

 section exists because it sounds thorough rather than because it changes the

 outcome, kill it.

 6. Fill the gaps a senior engineer would catch in a design review. What's

 missing that looks minor now and will hurt later? Edge cases, error handling,

 failure modes, prerequisites, migration paths, ordering constraints, and the

 easy-to-punt decisions that get expensive to reverse. Surface them now, while

 they're cheap.

 7. Rewrite the full plan from scratch. Do not patch the original — rebuild it

 with everything above baked in. Make it comprehensive, precise, and immediately

 actionable. Preserve the parts of the original that were already strong —

 rebuilding does not mean discarding what worked. The goal is the best possible

 plan, not the most rewritten one. Then, at the end, list the most important

 changes you made and explain the reasoning behind each one so I can judge

 whether your thinking holds up.

 Take your time. Think deeply. This is the version I'm going to execute from —

 make it the best one you're capable of producing.

 scanner is now actually live — you saw it enforce itself mid-session (and I investigated the apparent false positive, which turned out to be my own smoke test poisoning the violation log; lesson captured in

 the memory file).

 feedback_raw_pages_is_extraction_requests.md ). The redesign: when two extraction requests share a URL, MERGE their content instead of dropping one. Must measure verified_quality before enabling any flag that

 touches raw_pages. Cost target remains $25/week hard ceiling (memory: feedback_research_budget_25_per_week.md ), and S61 A/B ran at roughly 3x over that at $0.80/job blended (extrapolation from a handful of

 scanners.

 - LA archdiocese research restart. Secondary. Not until Tier 2 ships.

 - IN / OK / TN CSTA hand-curation. Roughly 15 matchable prospects (extrapolation from S57 missing-chapter audit). Per project_csta_roster_hand_curation_gaps.md .

 Tier 4 — deferred backlogs (don't touch without fresh sign-off)

 - 1,245 cold_license_request prospects — C4 backlog, stale-by-design.

 - 247 winback March prospects — stale-by-design, audit before approving per Session 59 intra_district lesson ( feedback_stale_backlogs_need_audit.md implied by narrative).

 Tier 5 — future wiring (architectural)

 - Wire prospect_loader.execute_load_plan into _on_prospect_research_complete so the research→loader handoff is automatic instead of manual per drip campaign. Not urgent — manual path works.

 ---

 Active kill switches (unchanged)

 - Rule scanner hooks: touch ~/.claude/state/scout-hooks-disabled — both hooks short-circuit at top. Now load-bearing: Commit 0 proved the scanner is really enforcing, and the production bug that silently

 disabled it is fixed.

 - Research Engine R1 flags: default OFF in ResearchJob.__init__ . Do not enable without a fresh plan.

 ---

 What I need from you before doing anything else

 Three decisions, in priority order:

 1. Confirm or overrule the no-pause call on the Wed cohort. Default is "no pause, let it ride."

 2. Is Session 63 still continuing, or are we at a stopping point? ctx marker shows 17% used, 33% to EOS — plenty of room. Tier 1 Task #2 (Philadelphia -1 investigation) is about 15 min estimated and cheap to

 do now; Tier 2 is plan-mode work and should get its own session.

 3. If we're continuing this session, do you want me to pick up Task #2 next, open a plan-mode session for Research Engine R1.1, or something else entirely?

 ⏺ Loading Enter Plan Mode and reading one more file to confirm the 1,245 answer.

 ⏺ 1,245 question — you're partly right

 The 1,119 prospects you remember are sequenced and done. Session 43 loaded them into the four C4 cold-license re-engagement sequences (by name — Teachers / District-Admin / Unknown-Role-School /

 Unknown-Role-District). That's a different cohort from the 1,245 in the current backlog.

 The 1,245 is a newer, separate cohort. Per SCOUT_PLAN.md , Session 59 counted the actual prospecting queue at 1,990 pending across all strategies. The breakdown: the five named strategies account for ~475, and

 1,245 cold_license_request + 247 winback are March 2026 accumulations that Scout's suggest_cold_license_requests scanner queued later but nothing ever pulled from. They sit in the pending queue, not in any

 sequence. They're deferred because (a) Session 59 marked most of Scout's March queue data as test-run scaffold and trust-questionable, and (b) a proper load would need the same care as the S43 push.

 So your intuition is right that "we sequenced cold license prospects" — we did, once, 1,119 of them. The 1,245 backlog is what accumulated after that initial push and was never triaged. Different people.

 Whether the 1,245 is worth pulling through before further work is exactly the kind of triage-vs-purge call that belongs in its own plan-mode session, not something to shoehorn into this one. I'll flag it in

 the plan I'm about to write but will not propose acting on it this session.

 ⏺ My recommendation on the Wed cadence

 Don't pause. A one-day shift on cold diocesan outreach doesn't materially change response dynamics, and pausing 15 individual sequenceStates via the API introduces a non-trivial touchpoint (pause + unpause + a

 second verify pass) with real breakage risk for marginal calendar-alignment gain. The Wed cohort just runs a day early; step cadences 5d/5d/6d/7d/8d still fire in sequence. Action: no pause. Let it ride. If

 you disagree, say the word and I'll walk back the decision.

 is correct when it's actually Thursday.

 Task #2 — Philadelphia verify -1 investigation. The writes landed cleanly (15 of 15 per the execute summary, sample verified against the POST logs). The verify code caught an exception from

 get_sequence_states(2008, include_prospect=False) and stamped -1 as a sentinel. Fastest triage: re-run --verify --force-day 2026-04-15 , read seq 2008 live count error: <exception> from the warning log, fix the

 client if it's a pagination issue, or accept it as a one-off rate-limit hiccup if it doesn't reproduce.

 early, Thu drip completed.

 Tier 2 — next plan-mode sessions (not today; each needs a dedicated session)

 Research Engine Round 1.1 — per-URL content MERGE, not dedup. S61 R1 plan failed because raw_pages is a list of extraction REQUESTS not unique pages, and URL dedup was lossy (memory:

 feedback_raw_pages_is_extraction_requests.md ). The redesign: when two extraction requests share a URL, MERGE their content instead of dropping one. Must measure verified_quality before enabling any flag that

 touches raw_pages. Cost target remains $25/week hard ceiling (memory: feedback_research_budget_25_per_week.md ), and S61 A/B ran at roughly 3x over that at $0.80/job blended (extrapolation from a handful of

 sample jobs, not a saturation measurement). Needs a full plan-mode session — self-pressure-test twice per Rule 4.

 BUG 5 code fix — tools/research_engine.py::_target_match_params . Runtime blocklist at memory/public_district_email_blocklist.json currently patches the shared-city contamination (Detroit row confirmed S59).

 The permanent fix is a proper target-match parameter rewrite. Also blocks: 9 pending dioceses review (Pittsburgh/OKC/Omaha et al) — 7 of 9 at risk per project_bug5_shared_city_gap.md . Plan-mode required.

 scanners.

 - IN / OK / TN CSTA hand-curation. Roughly 15 matchable prospects (extrapolation from S57 missing-chapter audit). Per project_csta_roster_hand_curation_gaps.md .

 ⏺ 1,245 question — you're partly right

 The 1,119 prospects you remember are sequenced and done. Session 43 loaded them into the four C4 cold-license re-engagement sequences (by name — Teachers / District-Admin / Unknown-Role-School /

 Unknown-Role-District). That's a different cohort from the 1,245 in the current backlog.

 The 1,245 is a newer, separate cohort. Per SCOUT_PLAN.md , Session 59 counted the actual prospecting queue at 1,990 pending across all strategies. The breakdown: the five named strategies account for ~475, and

 1,245 cold_license_request + 247 winback are March 2026 accumulations that Scout's suggest_cold_license_requests scanner queued later but nothing ever pulled from. They sit in the pending queue, not in any

 sequence. They're deferred because (a) Session 59 marked most of Scout's March queue data as test-run scaffold and trust-questionable, and (b) a proper load would need the same care as the S43 push.

 So your intuition is right that "we sequenced cold license prospects" — we did, once, 1,119 of them. The 1,245 backlog is what accumulated after that initial push and was never triaged. Different people.

 Whether the 1,245 is worth pulling through before further work is exactly the kind of triage-vs-purge call that belongs in its own plan-mode session, not something to shoehorn into this one. I'll flag it in

 the plan I'm about to write but will not propose acting on it this session.

 Plan: Session 63 knock-out — scanner hardening, verify fix, CSTA hand-curation, wrap

 Context — why this plan exists

 Session 62 built and shipped the rule scanner (R19 + R20) as a Claude Code hook pipeline but left Commit 0 empirical verification for Session 63. Session 63 so far:

 1. Finished Commit 0. Verified Stop- {decision:block} forces in-turn continuation by injecting the reason as a synthetic "Stop hook feedback: ..." user message; stop_hook_active: true is set on recursive fires,

 and the wrapper's early-exit guard is load-bearing. User Prompt Submit additional Context reaches Claude's next turn. The Stop stdin field is last_assistant_message , not last_message , and its contents are plain

 prose only — no tool-use JSON blocks.

 2. Uncovered and fixed a production bug in ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh . It was reading .last_message and has silently fail-opened every turn since the Session 62 install. Patched to

 .last_assistant_message . Commit f479241 records the findings in the scanner docstring.

 3. Loaded the Wednesday diocesan drip batch a day early on Tue 2026-04-14 per Steven's approval. Execute summary: 15 of 15 processed, 14 created, 1 existing reused, 0 failed, 0 skipped — all measured.

 4. Found two open issues that must be handled before end-of-session wrap:

 - Philadelphia verify -1 mismatch. diocesan_drip.py --verify --force-day 2026-04-15 showed get_sequence_states threw on the Philadelphia sequence only; writes were unaffected per the execute summary, but the

 read-path hiccup is a loose end in the verification tooling that will be used on Thursday's batch.

 - Rule scanner ghost-match false positives. The User Prompt Submit injector fired a correction directive citing three percent tokens ( 17%,40% 20% ) that I cannot locate in my actual recent text. Leading

 hypothesis: the violation log had stale entries from either Session 62 smoke tests or my own Session 63 smoke-test poisoning (the wrapper's hardcoded LOG path means any smoke test writes to the real production

 log). Hooks are currently disabled via kill switch ( ~/.claude/state/scout-hooks-disabled ) for the duration of plan-mode work, which is a regression from S62 and must be reversed before wrap.

 Steven's instruction for the rest of Session 63: knock out as much as possible in the order I recommend, EXCLUDING (a) Research Engine Round 1.1 planning, (b) BUG 5 fix in

 research_engine.py::_target_match_params , (c) Thursday diocesan drip (runs on actual Thu so step-1 timing doesn't compress), and (d) prospect_loader.execute_load_plan wiring into _on_prospect_research_complete

 — that handler lives at agent/main.py:319 on the 24/7 Railway path and is exactly the "non-trivial build" Rule 1 of CLAUDE.md says must have its own dedicated plan-mode session.

 Intended outcome. Session 63 ends with: scanner hooks live on a clean log and hardened against smoke-test poisoning; Philadelphia verify loose end closed or documented as a one-off; one or more CSTA chapter

 rosters hand-curated where public-district yield was previously zero; a clean EOS wrap in CLAUDE.md and SCOUT_HISTORY.md.

 Critical ordering constraint

 Task 1 must come first, and it must run while hooks are still disabled. Reason: Task 1 makes it safe to re-enable hooks, and subsequent tasks (especially Task 4's docs edits) will emit text that the live

 scanner will scan. If Task 1 is second, any false positive during Task 2 or Task 3 derails the session. Task 4 must come last because it depends on the outcomes of 1–3.

 Task 3 is the only task whose scope should flex based on context-window headroom at its start. Tasks 1, 2, and 4 are non-negotiable — Task 3 is compressible down to a single state or skipped entirely if

 headroom is tight.

 ---

 Task 1 — Scanner infrastructure hardening, diagnosis, and re-enable

 Non-negotiable. This task de-risks the rest of the session and reverses the kill-switch regression. Hard time budget: 20 minutes . If any step takes noticeably longer, document the blocker and fall back to the

 "minimum-viable re-enable" option at the bottom of this task.

 Step 1.1 — Verify the scanner itself is not buggy before touching the log (5 min)

 Before clearing the violation log on the assumption that stale entries are the root cause of the ghost 40% and 20% matches, prove the scanner's scan() function is actually behaving correctly against my recent

 output. If the scanner is buggy (e.g. normalization fails to strip backtick spans), a log clear papers over a real bug that will recur on the next session.

 Run the scanner directly against the three most recent assistant text blocks from the session's JSONL transcript at

 /Users/stevenadkins/.claude/projects/-Users-stevenadkins-Code-Scout/dc6f696a-2c23-482e-99ab-5e37304972e0.jsonl :

 - Block 27 = the big "Session 63 remaining work" plan response. Expected: 1 violation ( 17% at position around 3991). This was confirmed earlier this session.

 - Block 28 = the first correction response ( 17% (measured) + 33% (measured) ). Expected: 0 violations.

 - Block 29 = the second correction response with \ 17%` , `40%` , `20%`` in backtick spans. Expected: 0 violations (the normalizer strips backtick spans).

 stop and debug the normalizer before touching anything else; the whole plan depends on the scanner being trustworthy.

 Step 1.2 — Add SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG env override to both hook wrappers (5 min)

 This is the actual fix for the smoke-test log-poisoning trap documented in memory/feedback_rule_scanner_hook_installed.md .

 Edit /Users/stevenadkins/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh :

 Replace

 LOG=~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log

 with

 LOG="${SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG:-$HOME/.claude/state/scout-violations.log}"

 No other changes to the wrapper.

 Edit /Users/stevenadkins/.claude/hooks/scout-violation-inject.sh :

 Replace

 LOG=~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log

 PROC=~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log.processing

 with

 LOG="${SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG:-$HOME/.claude/state/scout-violations.log}"

 PROC="${LOG}.processing"

 Deriving PROC from LOG means the override is single-variable. Set SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG once in a test and both files honor it. No other changes.

 Validate the edits did not break bash syntax:

 bash -n ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh

 bash -n ~/.claude/hooks/scout-violation-inject.sh

 Both must exit 0.

 Step 1.3 — Clear stale production log state (1 min)

 rm -f ~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log ~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log.processing

 This is safe — the live session hooks are disabled, no active writer can race, and there is nothing in the log worth keeping.

 Step 1.4 — Full-pipeline smoke test using the env override (5 min)

 Four synthetic invocations against a temp log path, no production state touched.

 export SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG=/tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log

 rm -f /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log.processing

 # A. Bare percent triggers block + writes log

 echo '{"session_id":"t","last_assistant_message":"the answer is 17%","stop_hook_active":false}' \

 | bash ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh

 # Expect: block JSON on stdout; /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log has 1 line.

 # B. Labeled percent is clean

 echo '{"session_id":"t","last_assistant_message":"the answer is 17% (measured)","stop_hook_active":false}' \

 | bash ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh

 # Expect: empty stdout; /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log still has 1 line.

 # C. Recursion guard short-circuits

 echo '{"session_id":"t","last_assistant_message":"17% bare","stop_hook_active":true}' \

 | bash ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh

 # Expect: empty stdout; /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log still has 1 line.

 # D. Injector consumes the temp log

 echo '{}' | bash ~/.claude/hooks/scout-violation-inject.sh

 # Expect: JSON additionalContext on stdout mentioning "17%";

 # /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log absent; /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log.processing absent.

 # Verify production log is untouched throughout

 ls -la ~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log ~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log.processing 2>&1

 # Expect: both absent.

 unset SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG

 rm -f /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log.processing

 All four expectations must hit. If any fails, stop and diagnose before re-enabling hooks.

 Step 1.5 — Re-enable hooks and verify live (2 min)

 rm ~/.claude/state/scout-hooks-disabled

 ls ~/.claude/state/scout-hooks-disabled 2>&1 # Expect: "No such file or directory"

 are exempt (tool-use parameters, not assistant prose), but the closing response that calls Exit Plan Mode is scanned.

 shipped in Session 63 to both hook wrappers. Future smoke tests must set it; document the exact temp-path pattern from 1.4.

 Task 2 — Philadelphia -1 verify root cause (time-boxed, 10 min)

 Hard cap: 10 minutes. If root cause is not identified in 10 minutes, document as an S63 carryover and move on. The writes were verified via the execute summary (15 of 15 POSTed, all measured from the summary

 output), so this task closes a read-path loose end, not a data-integrity gap.

 Step 2.1 — Re-run verify and capture the exception (2 min)

 cd /Users/stevenadkins/Code/Scout

 .venv/bin/python scripts/diocesan_drip.py --verify --force-day 2026-04-15 2>&1 | tee /tmp/commit0/verify-run2.txt

 The caller in scripts/diocesan_drip.py:401 logs the exception via logger.warning(f"seq {sid} live count error: {e}") . Read the captured output and identify the exception type and message.

 Step 2.2 — Decision gate based on exception type (up to 5 min)

 - 429 / 500 / connection reset / any 5xx → transient, most likely resolved on the re-run. If the re-run passes, Task 2 is done — the original -1 was a one-off network/rate-limit hiccup. Move on.

 - Re-run also fails with the same reproducible exception → diagnose by reading tools/outreach_client.py:459-530 ( get_sequence_states ) with the specific exception in hand. The function does manual pagination

 with max_pages=100 and page[size]=50 , so a pagination ceiling, a malformed next link, or a JSON decode failure on a truncated page are the likely candidates. Implement the narrowest possible fix.

 - Exception is something unexpected (attribute error, type error, etc.) → this means a real bug in the client code path. Read, diagnose, fix. Add a single regression case if the fix is non-obvious.

 Step 2.3 — Fix (if needed, 3 min) or document (1 min)

 If a fix is made, re-run --verify --force-day 2026-04-15 one more time to confirm all six sequences match. Any code change goes into a surgical commit at the end of Task 4 with the other S63 commits.

 If no fix, add one line to CLAUDE.md CURRENT STATE in Task 4 noting that the Philadelphia verify was a one-off and re-runs cleanly, or that it is a reproducible client bug deferred to a carryover task.

 Files touched in Task 2 (only if a fix is needed):

 - /Users/stevenadkins/Code/Scout/tools/outreach_client.py — surgical change only.

 - Possibly scripts/test_outreach_validator.py or a new targeted test — only if fix is non-obvious and warrants regression coverage.

 Explicit non-goal. No rewrite of get_sequence_states . No pagination refactor. No retry framework. If the diagnosis points to any of those, document as carryover instead.

 Task 3 — CSTA hand-curation, compressible (hard cap 40 min)

 Compressible scope. Start with Indiana. Expand to Oklahoma only if IN is clean within 15 minutes of Task 3 start. Expand to Tennessee only if IN and OK are both clean within 25 minutes of Task 3 start. If ctx

 headroom is tight at Task 3 start (remaining EOS budget under a safe margin for Task 4's wrap), do IN only and defer OK + TN to a carryover task.

 Step 3.1 — Read prerequisites before touching anything (3 min)

 - Read memory/project_csta_roster_hand_curation_gaps.md for (a) the chapter URLs for IN / OK / TN, (b) the existing roster file path and its schema, (c) the prior S57 yield pattern that produced the "+15

 matchable" estimate.

 - Read tools/signal_processor.py::scan_csta_chapters to confirm (a) the function signature and whether states=None scans all or requires a list, (b) the actual cost per call (tools/CLAUDE.md claims ~$1.20/scan

 but the comment does not specify whether that's per state or per full run), (c) how the function consumes the roster seed file.

 If the roster file or scan function differs materially from assumptions, stop and re-scope Task 3. Do not proceed to data entry without understanding the consumption path.

 Step 3.2 — Indiana chapter roster (up to 12 min)

 - Use WebFetch to read the IN chapter's board/leadership page.

 - Extract board members: name, school/district affiliation, role, publicly-listed email only.

 - Skip entries without a public school-district affiliation (per feedback_scout_primary_target_is_public_districts.md ).

 - Skip duplicates of existing roster entries.

 - Write the new rows to the roster seed file using the existing schema.

 Step 3.3 — Oklahoma chapter roster (up to 10 min, conditional)

 Only if IN was clean and ctx headroom supports it. Same method.

 Step 3.4 — Tennessee chapter roster (up to 10 min, conditional)

 Only if IN + OK were clean and ctx headroom supports it. Same method.

 Step 3.5 — Run the CSTA scanner limited to curated states (5 min)

 .venv/bin/python -c "import tools.signal_processor as sp; r = sp.scan_csta_chapters(states=['IN'])" — or the correct state list depending on 3.2/3.3/3.4 completion. Capture the return: new rows written to the

 Signals tab, any parse errors.

 Cost note. Run this once, not per state iteration. Signals go to the Signals tab only — CSTA is signals-only per the signal_processor module docs, no auto-queue.

 Step 3.6 — Report yield (2 min)

 Tally: roster rows added per state (label as measured from the JSON diff), new signals written (label as measured from the scanner return), estimated cost (sample — from the per-run measurement once Task 3

 completes).

 - No scanner code changes.

 Explicit non-goal. No scanner code rewrite. No territory matching tweaks. No commitment to OK or TN before IN is clean.

 Time estimate

 - Task 1 (scanner hardening + diagnosis + re-enable): 20 min estimate, hard cap.

 - Task 2 (verify investigation): 10 min estimate, hard cap.

 Total: 65–85 minutes estimate. Current ctx headroom was 32% to EOS at the 11:33 marker (measured from the injected sidecar value); additional ctx has been consumed since by plan-mode exploration and this

 rewrite. Task 3 is compressible specifically to preserve headroom for Task 4, which is non-negotiable.

 Most important changes from the first draft of this plan

 1. Added Step 1.1 — verify the scanner is not buggy before clearing the log. The first draft assumed stale log entries without proving it. If the normalizer is buggy, a log clear is cosmetic and the false

 positive recurs. Scanning block 27/28/29 directly takes 5 minutes and gives either confidence or a real bug to fix — either outcome is better than the original "clear and hope."

 2. Tightened Task 2 to a hard 10-minute cap with a decision gate. The first draft's 20-minute budget was a middle ground that invites drift. Re-run + identify exception + fix-or-document is a binary — either

 it resolves fast or it's a deeper issue that doesn't belong in this session.

 3. Compressible Task 3 scope. The first draft promised three states in one session, which is unrealistic at current ctx headroom. IN first, OK and TN only if headroom, all three explicitly skippable. Added

 Step 3.1 to verify the scan_csta_chapters signature and cost model before committing to a run — neither was confirmed in the first draft.

 4. Added a minimum-viable re-enable fallback to Task 1. If Step 1.1 surfaces a real scanner bug or Step 1.4 fails, the plan still has a path to re-enable hooks by the end of the session instead of stalling.

 5. Specific verification commands throughout. The first draft's verification section listed categories ("hooks live with clean log"); the rewrite lists exact commands and expected outcomes. A senior reviewer

 executing this tomorrow should not have to invent anything.

 6. Removed the rollback section. Every rollback path in the first draft was either obvious ( git revert , rm , re-type the edit) or impossible to usefully pre-plan. Ceremony cut.

 7. Explicit exclusion of prospect_loader wiring with the Rule 1 reasoning. The first draft called it "risky" without citing the rule. Naming Rule 1 makes the exclusion a principled deferral, not a judgment

 call the reader has to trust.

 8. Explicit critical ordering constraint at the top. The first draft implied the ordering mattered; the rewrite states why and what breaks if it's changed.

 9. Expanded Context section. The first draft assumed the reader already understood the rule scanner pipeline and the wrapper bug. The rewrite walks through both so a fresh reader (or my future self in a later

 session) can execute from this file without cross-referencing.

 10. Shared env override between both wrappers, single variable. The first draft set LOG and PROC independently; the rewrite derives PROC from LOG so a test only has to set one env var. Small but meaningful

 ergonomics improvement that also guarantees the two files cannot drift.

 The 1,245 is a newer, separate cohort. Per SCOUT_PLAN.md , Session 59 counted the actual prospecting queue at 1,990 pending across all strategies. The breakdown: the five named strategies account for ~475, and

 1,245 cold_license_request + 247 winback are March 2026 accumulations that Scout's suggest_cold_license_requests scanner queued later but nothing ever pulled from. They sit in the pending queue, not in any

 sequence. They're deferred because (a) Session 59 marked most of Scout's March queue data as test-run scaffold and trust-questionable, and (b) a proper load would need the same care as the S43 push.

 So your intuition is right that "we sequenced cold license prospects" — we did, once, 1,119 of them. The 1,245 backlog is what accumulated after that initial push and was never triaged. Different people.

 Whether the 1,245 is worth pulling through before further work is exactly the kind of triage-vs-purge call that belongs in its own plan-mode session, not something to shoehorn into this one. I'll flag it in

 the plan I'm about to write but will not propose acting on it this session.

 Foundation holds. Four tasks in the order I proposed is still correct. Scanner hardening first because hooks are currently disabled and leaving them off at session end is a regression from Session 62's work —

 re-enabling without fixing the log-poisoning root cause guarantees the false-positive recurs. CSTA is primary-lane-compatible (public-school CS teachers reached via a different identification path), not the

 secondary-scanner drift Steven has been correcting me on.

 Genuine weaknesses I found:

 1. My diagnosis of the 40% / 20% ghosts is a guess, not a verified root cause. Before papering over with a log clear, I need to scan my own recent blocks through the rule scanner and prove it isn't a normalizer

 bug.

 2. Task 2's 20-min budget is loose. A verify investigation either resolves in one re-run or needs real debugging; the middle ground is the worst outcome. Tighten to a hard 10-min budget with a decision gate.

 3. Task 3's "30-60 min" is a wishful range. CSTA hand-curation across three states in a single session is not realistic given current ctx headroom — the honest plan is "IN first, expand to OK and TN only if

 headroom allows." Anything else is a promise I can't keep.

 4. Verification section is vague — needs exact commands, not categories.

 5. I never verified the scan_csta_chapters function signature or cost model. I'm assuming states=None means "all" and cost is per-scan — both need a 2-minute read before committing.

 6. The prospect_loader wiring deferral is correct but I should state the rule-1 reasoning explicitly so it's not just hand-waved.

 Plan saved to: ~/.claude/plans/flickering-nibbling-breeze.md · /plan to edit
 Plan: Session 63 knock-out — scanner hardening, verify fix, CSTA hand-curation, wrap

 Context — why this plan exists

 Session 62 built and shipped the rule scanner (R19 + R20) as a Claude Code hook pipeline but left Commit 0 empirical verification for Session 63. Session 63 so far:

 1. Finished Commit 0. Verified Stop- {decision:block} forces in-turn continuation by injecting the reason as a synthetic "Stop hook feedback: ..." user message; stop_hook_active: true is set on recursive
 fires, and the wrapper's early-exit guard is load-bearing. User Prompt Submit additional Context reaches Claude's next turn. The Stop stdin field is last_assistant_message , not last_message , and its contents

 are plain prose only — no tool-use JSON blocks.

 2. Uncovered and fixed a production bug in ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh . It was reading .last_message and has silently fail-opened every turn since the Session 62 install. Patched to

 .last_assistant_message . Commit f479241 records the findings in the scanner docstring.

 3. Loaded the Wednesday diocesan drip batch a day early on Tue 2026-04-14 per Steven's approval. Execute summary: 15 of 15 processed, 14 created, 1 existing reused, 0 failed, 0 skipped — all measured.

 4. Found two open issues that must be handled before end-of-session wrap:

 - Philadelphia verify -1 mismatch. diocesan_drip.py --verify --force-day 2026-04-15 showed get_sequence_states threw on the Philadelphia sequence only; writes were unaffected per the execute summary, but

 the read-path hiccup is a loose end in the verification tooling that will be used on Thursday's batch.

 - Rule scanner ghost-match false positives. The User Prompt Submit injector fired a correction directive citing three percent tokens ( 17%,40% 20% ) that I cannot locate in my actual recent text. Leading

 hypothesis: the violation log had stale entries from either Session 62 smoke tests or my own Session 63 smoke-test poisoning (the wrapper's hardcoded LOG path means any smoke test writes to the real

 production log). Hooks are currently disabled via kill switch ( ~/.claude/state/scout-hooks-disabled ) for the duration of plan-mode work, which is a regression from S62 and must be reversed before wrap.

 Steven's instruction for the rest of Session 63: knock out as much as possible in the order I recommend, EXCLUDING (a) Research Engine Round 1.1 planning, (b) BUG 5 fix in

 research_engine.py::_target_match_params , (c) Thursday diocesan drip (runs on actual Thu so step-1 timing doesn't compress), and (d) prospect_loader.execute_load_plan wiring into

 _on_prospect_research_complete — that handler lives at agent/main.py:319 on the 24/7 Railway path and is exactly the "non-trivial build" Rule 1 of CLAUDE.md says must have its own dedicated plan-mode

 session.

 Intended outcome. Session 63 ends with: scanner hooks live on a clean log and hardened against smoke-test poisoning; Philadelphia verify loose end closed or documented as a one-off; one or more CSTA chapter

 rosters hand-curated where public-district yield was previously zero; a clean EOS wrap in CLAUDE.md and SCOUT_HISTORY.md.

 Critical ordering constraint

 Task 1 must come first, and it must run while hooks are still disabled. Reason: Task 1 makes it safe to re-enable hooks, and subsequent tasks (especially Task 4's docs edits) will emit text that the live

 scanner will scan. If Task 1 is second, any false positive during Task 2 or Task 3 derails the session. Task 4 must come last because it depends on the outcomes of 1–3.

 Task 3 is the only task whose scope should flex based on context-window headroom at its start. Tasks 1, 2, and 4 are non-negotiable — Task 3 is compressible down to a single state or skipped entirely if

 headroom is tight.

 Task 1 — Scanner infrastructure hardening, diagnosis, and re-enable

 Non-negotiable. This task de-risks the rest of the session and reverses the kill-switch regression. Hard time budget: 20 minutes . If any step takes noticeably longer, document the blocker and fall back to

 the "minimum-viable re-enable" option at the bottom of this task.

 Step 1.1 — Verify the scanner itself is not buggy before touching the log (5 min)

 Before clearing the violation log on the assumption that stale entries are the root cause of the ghost 40% and 20% matches, prove the scanner's scan() function is actually behaving correctly against my

 recent output. If the scanner is buggy (e.g. normalization fails to strip backtick spans), a log clear papers over a real bug that will recur on the next session.

 Run the scanner directly against the three most recent assistant text blocks from the session's JSONL transcript at

 /Users/stevenadkins/.claude/projects/-Users-stevenadkins-Code-Scout/dc6f696a-2c23-482e-99ab-5e37304972e0.jsonl :

 - Block 27 = the big "Session 63 remaining work" plan response. Expected: 1 violation ( 17% at position around 3991). This was confirmed earlier this session.

 - Block 28 = the first correction response ( 17% (measured) + 33% (measured) ). Expected: 0 violations.

 - Block 29 = the second correction response with \ 17%` , `40%` , `20%`` in backtick spans. Expected: 0 violations (the normalizer strips backtick spans).

 — stop and debug the normalizer before touching anything else; the whole plan depends on the scanner being trustworthy.

 Step 1.2 — Add SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG env override to both hook wrappers (5 min)

 This is the actual fix for the smoke-test log-poisoning trap documented in memory/feedback_rule_scanner_hook_installed.md .

 Edit /Users/stevenadkins/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh :

 Replace

 LOG=~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log

 with

 LOG="${SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG:-$HOME/.claude/state/scout-violations.log}"

 No other changes to the wrapper.

 Edit /Users/stevenadkins/.claude/hooks/scout-violation-inject.sh :

 Replace

 LOG=~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log

 PROC=~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log.processing

 with

 LOG="${SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG:-$HOME/.claude/state/scout-violations.log}"

 PROC="${LOG}.processing"

 Deriving PROC from LOG means the override is single-variable. Set SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG once in a test and both files honor it. No other changes.

 Validate the edits did not break bash syntax:

 bash -n ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh

 bash -n ~/.claude/hooks/scout-violation-inject.sh

 Both must exit 0.

 Step 1.3 — Clear stale production log state (1 min)

 rm -f ~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log ~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log.processing

 This is safe — the live session hooks are disabled, no active writer can race, and there is nothing in the log worth keeping.

 Step 1.4 — Full-pipeline smoke test using the env override (5 min)

 Four synthetic invocations against a temp log path, no production state touched.

 export SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG=/tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log

 rm -f /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log.processing

 # A. Bare percent triggers block + writes log

 echo '{"session_id":"t","last_assistant_message":"the answer is 17%","stop_hook_active":false}' \

 | bash ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh

 # Expect: block JSON on stdout; /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log has 1 line.

 # B. Labeled percent is clean

 echo '{"session_id":"t","last_assistant_message":"the answer is 17% (measured)","stop_hook_active":false}' \

 | bash ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh

 # Expect: empty stdout; /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log still has 1 line.

 # C. Recursion guard short-circuits

 echo '{"session_id":"t","last_assistant_message":"17% bare","stop_hook_active":true}' \

 | bash ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh

 # Expect: empty stdout; /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log still has 1 line.

 # D. Injector consumes the temp log

 echo '{}' | bash ~/.claude/hooks/scout-violation-inject.sh

 # Expect: JSON additionalContext on stdout mentioning "17%";

 # /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log absent; /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log.processing absent.

 # Verify production log is untouched throughout

 ls -la ~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log ~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log.processing 2>&1

 # Expect: both absent.

 unset SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG

 rm -f /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log.processing

 All four expectations must hit. If any fails, stop and diagnose before re-enabling hooks.

 Step 1.5 — Re-enable hooks and verify live (2 min)

 rm ~/.claude/state/scout-hooks-disabled

 ls ~/.claude/state/scout-hooks-disabled 2>&1 # Expect: "No such file or directory"

 From this point, Session 63 has live R19 + R20 enforcement with a clean log. Every subsequent assistant response must label numerical claims within 100 characters of the value. Plan-mode writes to this file

 are exempt (tool-use parameters, not assistant prose), but the closing response that calls Exit Plan Mode is scanned.

 Step 1.6 — Update the memory file (2 min)

 Append to the "Smoke-test log-poisoning trap" section in ~/.claude/projects/-Users-stevenadkins-Code-Scout/memory/feedback_rule_scanner_hook_installed.md : the fix is the SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG env override,

 shipped in Session 63 to both hook wrappers. Future smoke tests must set it; document the exact temp-path pattern from 1.4.

 Minimum-viable re-enable (fallback if Task 1 is blocked)

 If Step 1.1 reveals a real scanner bug or Step 1.4 fails unexpectedly, the fallback is:

 1. Keep the wrapper edits from 1.2 (they are strictly additive and safe).

 2. Clear the log per 1.3.

 3. Skip 1.4's full-pipeline test and instead run a single invocation: echo '{"last_assistant_message":"test","stop_hook_active":false}' | bash ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh — expect empty stdout (clean

 text, no digits, pre-filter short-circuit).

 Task 2 — Philadelphia -1 verify root cause (time-boxed, 10 min)

 Hard cap: 10 minutes. If root cause is not identified in 10 minutes, document as an S63 carryover and move on. The writes were verified via the execute summary (15 of 15 POSTed, all measured from the

 summary output), so this task closes a read-path loose end, not a data-integrity gap.

 Step 2.1 — Re-run verify and capture the exception (2 min)

 cd /Users/stevenadkins/Code/Scout

 .venv/bin/python scripts/diocesan_drip.py --verify --force-day 2026-04-15 2>&1 | tee /tmp/commit0/verify-run2.txt

 The caller in scripts/diocesan_drip.py:401 logs the exception via logger.warning(f"seq {sid} live count error: {e}") . Read the captured output and identify the exception type and message.

 Step 2.2 — Decision gate based on exception type (up to 5 min)

 - 429 / 500 / connection reset / any 5xx → transient, most likely resolved on the re-run. If the re-run passes, Task 2 is done — the original -1 was a one-off network/rate-limit hiccup. Move on.

 - Re-run also fails with the same reproducible exception → diagnose by reading tools/outreach_client.py:459-530 ( get_sequence_states ) with the specific exception in hand. The function does manual pagination

 with max_pages=100 and page[size]=50 , so a pagination ceiling, a malformed next link, or a JSON decode failure on a truncated page are the likely candidates. Implement the narrowest possible fix.

 - Exception is something unexpected (attribute error, type error, etc.) → this means a real bug in the client code path. Read, diagnose, fix. Add a single regression case if the fix is non-obvious.

 Step 2.3 — Fix (if needed, 3 min) or document (1 min)

 If a fix is made, re-run --verify --force-day 2026-04-15 one more time to confirm all six sequences match. Any code change goes into a surgical commit at the end of Task 4 with the other S63 commits.

 If no fix, add one line to CLAUDE.md CURRENT STATE in Task 4 noting that the Philadelphia verify was a one-off and re-runs cleanly, or that it is a reproducible client bug deferred to a carryover task.

 Files touched in Task 2 (only if a fix is needed):

 - /Users/stevenadkins/Code/Scout/tools/outreach_client.py — surgical change only.

 - Possibly scripts/test_outreach_validator.py or a new targeted test — only if fix is non-obvious and warrants regression coverage.

 Explicit non-goal. No rewrite of get_sequence_states . No pagination refactor. No retry framework. If the diagnosis points to any of those, document as carryover instead.

 Compressible scope. Start with Indiana. Expand to Oklahoma only if IN is clean within 15 minutes of Task 3 start. Expand to Tennessee only if IN and OK are both clean within 25 minutes of Task 3 start. If

 ctx headroom is tight at Task 3 start (remaining EOS budget under a safe margin for Task 4's wrap), do IN only and defer OK + TN to a carryover task.

 matchable" estimate.

 ~$1.20/scan but the comment does not specify whether that's per state or per full run), (c) how the function consumes the roster seed file.

 Step 3.2 — Indiana chapter roster (up to 12 min)

 Only if IN was clean and ctx headroom supports it. Same method.

 Step 3.4 — Tennessee chapter roster (up to 10 min, conditional)

 Only if IN + OK were clean and ctx headroom supports it. Same method.

 the Signals tab, any parse errors.

 Time estimate

 - Task 1 (scanner hardening + diagnosis + re-enable): 20 min estimate, hard cap.

 - Task 2 (verify investigation): 10 min estimate, hard cap.

 rewrite. Task 3 is compressible specifically to preserve headroom for Task 4, which is non-negotiable.

 Most important changes from the first draft of this plan

 1. Added Step 1.1 — verify the scanner is not buggy before clearing the log. The first draft assumed stale log entries without proving it. If the normalizer is buggy, a log clear is cosmetic and the false

 positive recurs. Scanning block 27/28/29 directly takes 5 minutes and gives either confidence or a real bug to fix — either outcome is better than the original "clear and hope."

 2. Tightened Task 2 to a hard 10-minute cap with a decision gate. The first draft's 20-minute budget was a middle ground that invites drift. Re-run + identify exception + fix-or-document is a binary —

 either it resolves fast or it's a deeper issue that doesn't belong in this session.

 3. Compressible Task 3 scope. The first draft promised three states in one session, which is unrealistic at current ctx headroom. IN first, OK and TN only if headroom, all three explicitly skippable. Added

 Step 3.1 to verify the scan_csta_chapters signature and cost model before committing to a run — neither was confirmed in the first draft.

 4. Added a minimum-viable re-enable fallback to Task 1. If Step 1.1 surfaces a real scanner bug or Step 1.4 fails, the plan still has a path to re-enable hooks by the end of the session instead of stalling.

 5. Specific verification commands throughout. The first draft's verification section listed categories ("hooks live with clean log"); the rewrite lists exact commands and expected outcomes. A senior

 reviewer executing this tomorrow should not have to invent anything.

 6. Removed the rollback section. Every rollback path in the first draft was either obvious ( git revert , rm , re-type the edit) or impossible to usefully pre-plan. Ceremony cut.

 7. Explicit exclusion of prospect_loader wiring with the Rule 1 reasoning. The first draft called it "risky" without citing the rule. Naming Rule 1 makes the exclusion a principled deferral, not a judgment

 call the reader has to trust.

 8. Explicit critical ordering constraint at the top. The first draft implied the ordering mattered; the rewrite states why and what breaks if it's changed.

 9. Expanded Context section. The first draft assumed the reader already understood the rule scanner pipeline and the wrapper bug. The rewrite walks through both so a fresh reader (or my future self in a

 later session) can execute from this file without cross-referencing.

 10. Shared env override between both wrappers, single variable. The first draft set LOG and PROC independently; the rewrite derives PROC from LOG so a test only has to set one env var. Small but meaningful

 ergonomics improvement that also guarantees the two files cannot drift.

 ⏺ 1,245 question — you're partly right

 The 1,119 prospects you remember are sequenced and done. Session 43 loaded them into the four C4 cold-license re-engagement sequences (by name — Teachers / District-Admin / Unknown-Role-School /

 Unknown-Role-District). That's a different cohort from the 1,245 in the current backlog.

 The 1,245 is a newer, separate cohort. Per SCOUT_PLAN.md , Session 59 counted the actual prospecting queue at 1,990 pending across all strategies. The breakdown: the five named strategies account for ~475, and

 1,245 cold_license_request + 247 winback are March 2026 accumulations that Scout's suggest_cold_license_requests scanner queued later but nothing ever pulled from. They sit in the pending queue, not in any

 sequence. They're deferred because (a) Session 59 marked most of Scout's March queue data as test-run scaffold and trust-questionable, and (b) a proper load would need the same care as the S43 push.

 So your intuition is right that "we sequenced cold license prospects" — we did, once, 1,119 of them. The 1,245 backlog is what accumulated after that initial push and was never triaged. Different people.

 Whether the 1,245 is worth pulling through before further work is exactly the kind of triage-vs-purge call that belongs in its own plan-mode session, not something to shoehorn into this one. I'll flag it in

 the plan I'm about to write but will not propose acting on it this session.

 ⏺ Thinking through the pressure-test.

 Foundation holds. Four tasks in the order I proposed is still correct. Scanner hardening first because hooks are currently disabled and leaving them off at session end is a regression from Session 62's work —

 re-enabling without fixing the log-poisoning root cause guarantees the false-positive recurs. CSTA is primary-lane-compatible (public-school CS teachers reached via a different identification path), not the

 secondary-scanner drift Steven has been correcting me on.

 Genuine weaknesses I found:

 1. My diagnosis of the 40% / 20% ghosts is a guess, not a verified root cause. Before papering over with a log clear, I need to scan my own recent blocks through the rule scanner and prove it isn't a normalizer

 bug.

 2. Task 2's 20-min budget is loose. A verify investigation either resolves in one re-run or needs real debugging; the middle ground is the worst outcome. Tighten to a hard 10-min budget with a decision gate.

 3. Task 3's "30-60 min" is a wishful range. CSTA hand-curation across three states in a single session is not realistic given current ctx headroom — the honest plan is "IN first, expand to OK and TN only if

 headroom allows." Anything else is a promise I can't keep.

 4. Verification section is vague — needs exact commands, not categories.

 5. I never verified the scan_csta_chapters function signature or cost model. I'm assuming states=None means "all" and cost is per-scan — both need a 2-minute read before committing.

 6. The prospect_loader wiring deferral is correct but I should state the rule-1 reasoning explicitly so it's not just hand-waved.

 7. Rollback section is ceremony — for each task, rollback is either obvious ( rm a log, git revert a commit, re-type a one-line edit) or impossible to meaningfully write up in advance. Cut it.

 ⏺ Updated plan

 ⎿  /plan to preview

 ⏺ User approved Claude's plan

 ⎿  Plan saved to: ~/.claude/plans/flickering-nibbling-breeze.md · /plan to edit

 Plan: Session 63 knock-out — scanner hardening, verify fix, CSTA hand-curation, wrap

 Context — why this plan exists

 Session 62 built and shipped the rule scanner (R19 + R20) as a Claude Code hook pipeline but left Commit 0 empirical verification for Session 63. Session 63 so far:

 1. Finished Commit 0. Verified Stop- {decision:block} forces in-turn continuation by injecting the reason as a synthetic "Stop hook feedback: ..." user message; stop_hook_active: true is set on recursive

 fires, and the wrapper's early-exit guard is load-bearing. User Prompt Submit additional Context reaches Claude's next turn. The Stop stdin field is last_assistant_message , not last_message , and its contents

 are plain prose only — no tool-use JSON blocks.

 2. Uncovered and fixed a production bug in ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh . It was reading .last_message and has silently fail-opened every turn since the Session 62 install. Patched to

 .last_assistant_message . Commit f479241 records the findings in the scanner docstring.

 3. Loaded the Wednesday diocesan drip batch a day early on Tue 2026-04-14 per Steven's approval. Execute summary: 15 of 15 processed, 14 created, 1 existing reused, 0 failed, 0 skipped — all measured.

 4. Found two open issues that must be handled before end-of-session wrap:

 - Philadelphia verify -1 mismatch. diocesan_drip.py --verify --force-day 2026-04-15 showed get_sequence_states threw on the Philadelphia sequence only; writes were unaffected per the execute summary, but

 the read-path hiccup is a loose end in the verification tooling that will be used on Thursday's batch.

 - Rule scanner ghost-match false positives. The User Prompt Submit injector fired a correction directive citing three percent tokens ( 17%,40% 20% ) that I cannot locate in my actual recent text. Leading

 hypothesis: the violation log had stale entries from either Session 62 smoke tests or my own Session 63 smoke-test poisoning (the wrapper's hardcoded LOG path means any smoke test writes to the real

 production log). Hooks are currently disabled via kill switch ( ~/.claude/state/scout-hooks-disabled ) for the duration of plan-mode work, which is a regression from S62 and must be reversed before wrap.

 Steven's instruction for the rest of Session 63: knock out as much as possible in the order I recommend, EXCLUDING (a) Research Engine Round 1.1 planning, (b) BUG 5 fix in

 research_engine.py::_target_match_params , (c) Thursday diocesan drip (runs on actual Thu so step-1 timing doesn't compress), and (d) prospect_loader.execute_load_plan wiring into

 _on_prospect_research_complete — that handler lives at agent/main.py:319 on the 24/7 Railway path and is exactly the "non-trivial build" Rule 1 of CLAUDE.md says must have its own dedicated plan-mode

 session.

 Intended outcome. Session 63 ends with: scanner hooks live on a clean log and hardened against smoke-test poisoning; Philadelphia verify loose end closed or documented as a one-off; one or more CSTA chapter

 rosters hand-curated where public-district yield was previously zero; a clean EOS wrap in CLAUDE.md and SCOUT_HISTORY.md.

 Critical ordering constraint

 Task 1 must come first, and it must run while hooks are still disabled. Reason: Task 1 makes it safe to re-enable hooks, and subsequent tasks (especially Task 4's docs edits) will emit text that the live

 scanner will scan. If Task 1 is second, any false positive during Task 2 or Task 3 derails the session. Task 4 must come last because it depends on the outcomes of 1–3.

 Task 3 is the only task whose scope should flex based on context-window headroom at its start. Tasks 1, 2, and 4 are non-negotiable — Task 3 is compressible down to a single state or skipped entirely if

 headroom is tight.

 ---

 Task 1 — Scanner infrastructure hardening, diagnosis, and re-enable

 Non-negotiable. This task de-risks the rest of the session and reverses the kill-switch regression. Hard time budget: 20 minutes . If any step takes noticeably longer, document the blocker and fall back to

 the "minimum-viable re-enable" option at the bottom of this task.

 Step 1.1 — Verify the scanner itself is not buggy before touching the log (5 min)

 Before clearing the violation log on the assumption that stale entries are the root cause of the ghost 40% and 20% matches, prove the scanner's scan() function is actually behaving correctly against my

 recent output. If the scanner is buggy (e.g. normalization fails to strip backtick spans), a log clear papers over a real bug that will recur on the next session.

 Run the scanner directly against the three most recent assistant text blocks from the session's JSONL transcript at

 /Users/stevenadkins/.claude/projects/-Users-stevenadkins-Code-Scout/dc6f696a-2c23-482e-99ab-5e37304972e0.jsonl :

 - Block 27 = the big "Session 63 remaining work" plan response. Expected: 1 violation ( 17% at position around 3991). This was confirmed earlier this session.

 - Block 28 = the first correction response ( 17% (measured) + 33% (measured) ). Expected: 0 violations.

 - Block 29 = the second correction response with \ 17%` , `40%` , `20%`` in backtick spans. Expected: 0 violations (the normalizer strips backtick spans).

 — stop and debug the normalizer before touching anything else; the whole plan depends on the scanner being trustworthy.

 Step 1.2 — Add SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG env override to both hook wrappers (5 min)

 This is the actual fix for the smoke-test log-poisoning trap documented in memory/feedback_rule_scanner_hook_installed.md .

 Edit /Users/stevenadkins/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh :

 Replace

 LOG=~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log

 with

 LOG="${SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG:-$HOME/.claude/state/scout-violations.log}"

 No other changes to the wrapper.

 Edit /Users/stevenadkins/.claude/hooks/scout-violation-inject.sh :

 Replace

 LOG=~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log

 PROC=~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log.processing

 with

 LOG="${SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG:-$HOME/.claude/state/scout-violations.log}"

 PROC="${LOG}.processing"

 Deriving PROC from LOG means the override is single-variable. Set SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG once in a test and both files honor it. No other changes.

 Validate the edits did not break bash syntax:

 bash -n ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh

 bash -n ~/.claude/hooks/scout-violation-inject.sh

 Both must exit 0.

 Step 1.3 — Clear stale production log state (1 min)

 rm -f ~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log ~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log.processing

 This is safe — the live session hooks are disabled, no active writer can race, and there is nothing in the log worth keeping.

 Step 1.4 — Full-pipeline smoke test using the env override (5 min)

 Four synthetic invocations against a temp log path, no production state touched.

 export SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG=/tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log

 rm -f /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log.processing

 # A. Bare percent triggers block + writes log

 echo '{"session_id":"t","last_assistant_message":"the answer is 17%","stop_hook_active":false}' \

 | bash ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh

 # Expect: block JSON on stdout; /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log has 1 line.

 # B. Labeled percent is clean

 echo '{"session_id":"t","last_assistant_message":"the answer is 17% (measured)","stop_hook_active":false}' \

 | bash ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh

 # Expect: empty stdout; /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log still has 1 line.

 # C. Recursion guard short-circuits

 echo '{"session_id":"t","last_assistant_message":"17% bare","stop_hook_active":true}' \

 | bash ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh

 # Expect: empty stdout; /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log still has 1 line.

 # D. Injector consumes the temp log

 echo '{}' | bash ~/.claude/hooks/scout-violation-inject.sh

 # Expect: JSON additionalContext on stdout mentioning "17%";

 # /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log absent; /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log.processing absent.

 # Verify production log is untouched throughout

 ls -la ~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log ~/.claude/state/scout-violations.log.processing 2>&1

 # Expect: both absent.

 unset SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG

 rm -f /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log /tmp/commit0/smoke-vlog.log.processing

 All four expectations must hit. If any fails, stop and diagnose before re-enabling hooks.

 Step 1.5 — Re-enable hooks and verify live (2 min)

 rm ~/.claude/state/scout-hooks-disabled

 ls ~/.claude/state/scout-hooks-disabled 2>&1 # Expect: "No such file or directory"

 From this point, Session 63 has live R19 + R20 enforcement with a clean log. Every subsequent assistant response must label numerical claims within 100 characters of the value. Plan-mode writes to this file

 are exempt (tool-use parameters, not assistant prose), but the closing response that calls Exit Plan Mode is scanned.

 Step 1.6 — Update the memory file (2 min)

 Append to the "Smoke-test log-poisoning trap" section in ~/.claude/projects/-Users-stevenadkins-Code-Scout/memory/feedback_rule_scanner_hook_installed.md : the fix is the SCOUT_VIOLATIONS_LOG env override,

 shipped in Session 63 to both hook wrappers. Future smoke tests must set it; document the exact temp-path pattern from 1.4.

 Minimum-viable re-enable (fallback if Task 1 is blocked)

 If Step 1.1 reveals a real scanner bug or Step 1.4 fails unexpectedly, the fallback is:

 1. Keep the wrapper edits from 1.2 (they are strictly additive and safe).

 2. Clear the log per 1.3.

 3. Skip 1.4's full-pipeline test and instead run a single invocation: echo '{"last_assistant_message":"test","stop_hook_active":false}' | bash ~/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh — expect empty stdout (clean

 text, no digits, pre-filter short-circuit).

 4. Re-enable per 1.5.

 5. Document the scanner bug as an S63 carryover in Task 4's EOS wrap.

 This preserves the re-enable outcome even if the diagnostic step surfaces a deeper problem.

 Files touched in Task 1:

 - /Users/stevenadkins/.claude/hooks/scout-stop-scan.sh

 - /Users/stevenadkins/.claude/hooks/scout-violation-inject.sh

 - /Users/stevenadkins/.claude/projects/-Users-stevenadkins-Code-Scout/memory/feedback_rule_scanner_hook_installed.md

 - None of the Scout repo (all machine-local).

 ---

 Task 2 — Philadelphia -1 verify root cause (time-boxed, 10 min)

 Hard cap: 10 minutes. If root cause is not identified in 10 minutes, document as an S63 carryover and move on. The writes were verified via the execute summary (15 of 15 POSTed, all measured from the

 summary output), so this task closes a read-path loose end, not a data-integrity gap.

 Step 2.1 — Re-run verify and capture the exception (2 min)

 cd /Users/stevenadkins/Code/Scout

 .venv/bin/python scripts/diocesan_drip.py --verify --force-day 2026-04-15 2>&1 | tee /tmp/commit0/verify-run2.txt

 The caller in scripts/diocesan_drip.py:401 logs the exception via logger.warning(f"seq {sid} live count error: {e}") . Read the captured output and identify the exception type and message.

 Step 2.2 — Decision gate based on exception type (up to 5 min)

 - 429 / 500 / connection reset / any 5xx → transient, most likely resolved on the re-run. If the re-run passes, Task 2 is done — the original -1 was a one-off network/rate-limit hiccup. Move on.

 - Re-run also fails with the same reproducible exception → diagnose by reading tools/outreach_client.py:459-530 ( get_sequence_states ) with the specific exception in hand. The function does manual pagination

 with max_pages=100 and page[size]=50 , so a pagination ceiling, a malformed next link, or a JSON decode failure on a truncated page are the likely candidates. Implement the narrowest possible fix.

 - Exception is something unexpected (attribute error, type error, etc.) → this means a real bug in the client code path. Read, diagnose, fix. Add a single regression case if the fix is non-obvious.

 Step 2.3 — Fix (if needed, 3 min) or document (1 min)

 If a fix is made, re-run --verify --force-day 2026-04-15 one more time to confirm all six sequences match. Any code change goes into a surgical commit at the end of Task 4 with the other S63 commits.

 If no fix, add one line to CLAUDE.md CURRENT STATE in Task 4 noting that the Philadelphia verify was a one-off and re-runs cleanly, or that it is a reproducible client bug deferred to a carryover task.

 Files touched in Task 2 (only if a fix is needed):

 - /Users/stevenadkins/Code/Scout/tools/outreach_client.py — surgical change only.

 - Possibly scripts/test_outreach_validator.py or a new targeted test — only if fix is non-obvious and warrants regression coverage.

 Explicit non-goal. No rewrite of get_sequence_states . No pagination refactor. No retry framework. If the diagnosis points to any of those, document as carryover instead.

 ---

 Task 3 — CSTA hand-curation, compressible (hard cap 40 min)

 Compressible scope. Start with Indiana. Expand to Oklahoma only if IN is clean within 15 minutes of Task 3 start. Expand to Tennessee only if IN and OK are both clean within 25 minutes of Task 3 start. If

 ctx headroom is tight at Task 3 start (remaining EOS budget under a safe margin for Task 4's wrap), do IN only and defer OK + TN to a carryover task.

 Step 3.1 — Read prerequisites before touching anything (3 min)

 - Read memory/project_csta_roster_hand_curation_gaps.md for (a) the chapter URLs for IN / OK / TN, (b) the existing roster file path and its schema, (c) the prior S57 yield pattern that produced the "+15

 matchable" estimate.

 - Read tools/signal_processor.py::scan_csta_chapters to confirm (a) the function signature and whether states=None scans all or requires a list, (b) the actual cost per call (tools/CLAUDE.md claims

 ~$1.20/scan but the comment does not specify whether that's per state or per full run), (c) how the function consumes the roster seed file.

 If the roster file or scan function differs materially from assumptions, stop and re-scope Task 3. Do not proceed to data entry without understanding the consumption path.

 Step 3.2 — Indiana chapter roster (up to 12 min)

 - Use WebFetch to read the IN chapter's board/leadership page.

 - Extract board members: name, school/district affiliation, role, publicly-listed email only.

 - Skip entries without a public school-district affiliation (per feedback_scout_primary_target_is_public_districts.md ).

 - Skip duplicates of existing roster entries.

 - Write the new rows to the roster seed file using the existing schema.

 Step 3.3 — Oklahoma chapter roster (up to 10 min, conditional)

 Only if IN was clean and ctx headroom supports it. Same method.

 Step 3.4 — Tennessee chapter roster (up to 10 min, conditional)

 Only if IN + OK were clean and ctx headroom supports it. Same method.

 Step 3.5 — Run the CSTA scanner limited to curated states (5 min)

 .venv/bin/python -c "import tools.signal_processor as sp; r = sp.scan_csta_chapters(states=['IN'])" — or the correct state list depending on 3.2/3.3/3.4 completion. Capture the return: new rows written to

 the Signals tab, any parse errors.

 Cost note. Run this once, not per state iteration. Signals go to the Signals tab only — CSTA is signals-only per the signal_processor module docs, no auto-queue.

 Step 3.6 — Report yield (2 min)

 Tally: roster rows added per state (label as measured from the JSON diff), new signals written (label as measured from the scanner return), estimated cost (sample — from the per-run measurement once Task 3

 completes).

 Explicit non-goal. No scanner code rewrite. No territory matching tweaks. No commitment to OK or TN before IN is clean.

 Every session ends with a clean wrap. This task is non-negotiable and must start with at least the EOS budget as ctx headroom. If Task 3 starts eating into the wrap budget, truncate Task 3 immediately.

 One commit per logical change (per CLAUDE.md critical rule 8 — multi-feature sessions ship one commit per feature for surgical rollback). Candidates:
